turns.content: I've been reading about the no_std feature flag but I'm still completely lost on what exactly gets removed. The docs say it removes the standard library, so I assume things like String or Vec disappear, but how do I write code that doesn't rely on heap allocation or global state?

That's a great starting point because you're right about what disappears. When you enable no_std, the standard library crates like std::collections::{Vec, HashMap} and std::string::{String, Str} are no longer available in your module scope. Instead, you must import primitives directly from core::{alloc, str, vec} or use core::aliases to simulate types that look familiar but don't actually depend on the full standard library.

Oh I see so I can still use Vec and String if I import them from core? But what about panic handling? In my current project I'm getting a compile error saying no panic handler is found, which makes sense because std::panic isn't available anymore.

Exactly. Without the standard library's panic infrastructure you cannot rely on automatic unwinding or default handlers. You must implement a custom panic hook at runtime using core::panicking::{set_hook, get_hook}. This allows your application to recover gracefully or exit cleanly depending on your needs.

I understand the panic hook part now but I'm still confused about memory management. If there's no global heap like in std what happens when I allocate? Can't I just use a static buffer or manual allocation?

You are absolutely correct on that point because the core crate provides the foundational types for low-level memory operations. You can implement your own allocator using core::alloc::{Global, Box, Vec} or use existing allocators like embedded_alloc which wraps these primitives to provide safe abstractions without requiring external dependencies.
Wait I think I'm getting somewhere but what about threading? Can't I still spawn threads if I don't have std::thread?

You cannot use standard library threading primitives like std::thread or tokio's runtime because they depend on the full standard library. Instead you must implement a custom thread scheduler using core::sync::{Mutex, Barrier} and atomic types to coordinate tasks manually.
I see so I can't even use File or network sockets? Those seem essential for most applications. Is there any way to get file access without std?

You cannot directly access the filesystem because core does not include the necessary abstractions like std::fs::{File, Read}. Instead you must implement your own I/O layer using raw system calls through core::arch or use a crate like esp-backtrace for specific platforms that provide these low-level interfaces.

This is getting quite complex but I think I finally understand why no_std is so strict. So essentially it forces me to build everything from scratch using only core primitives? And this makes sense for embedded systems where we need total control over memory and resources.

That's exactly right because the entire point of no_std is to strip away everything that depends on an operating system or global state. By forcing you to use only core primitives you gain complete control over every byte allocated and every operation performed.
One last thing I'm wondering about the difference between using no_std in a library crate versus a binary crate. Does it really matter? Can't I just use either?

There is a significant difference because libraries often need to be reusable across different platforms including those with full standard libraries available, while binaries are typically target-specific like microcontrollers where you absolutely require no_std for maximum performance and control.
